mnemonics: H0: No Nonsense Hypothesis (Null hypothesis is a statement of 'no effect' or 'no difference')
P-Hacking Patrol: Always set your significance level α BEFORE you run the test to avoid bias.
TYPE I vs TYPE II: A Type I error is a 'False Alarm' (rejecting a true H0). A Type II error is a 'Miss' (failing to reject a false H0).
keyPoints: The null hypothesis (H0) is the default assumption to be tested, often representing no change or no effect.
The p-value is the probability of obtaining results at least as extreme as the observed results, assuming the null hypothesis is true.
A result is statistically significant if the p-value is less than the predetermined significance level (α), leading to the rejection of H0.
commonMistakes: Concluding that a small p-value proves the alternative hypothesis is true; it only indicates the data is inconsistent with the null hypothesis.
Failing to consider the possibility of a Type II error (false negative) when a test fails to reject the null hypothesis.
Using a one-tailed test when a two-tailed test is more appropriate, often to artificially lower the p-value and achieve significance.
